channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Warhammer 40,000 Armageddon Review - Space Marines Vs Orks In All-Out Combat
item.content:encoded:
A new edition of Warhammer 40,000 has been released, and as always, the new edition launches with an explosive new box featuring the story of Operation Imperator and miniatures from the Space Marines and Orks that will wage war against one another.

What's In The Warhammer 40,000 Armageddon Box?

Inside Warhammer 40,000 Armageddon, you'll find the following:

  • Armageddon: Operation Imperator book detailing the lore of the campaign and the two enemy factions (Exclusive to this box set)
  • 61 different miniatures comprising both the Space Marine and Ork armies, these miniatures are push-fit for new players' ease (and honestly, returning players too)
    • 23 miniatures for the Space Marines
    • 38 miniatures for the Orks
  • An assembly book with instructions on how to assemble the two armies
  • The Core Rules booklet to teach you the game, at a size to make it readily available
  • Dominatus Narrative Campaign Deck
  • Chapter Approved 2026-2027 Mission Deck
  • 17 Datasheet cards for each of the unit types included in the box
  • Transfer sheets for model icons and symbols

This box will essentially feature everything a returning or new player will need to start an Armageddon campaign. Inside this tome, you'll get caught up on the lore from Armageddon: The Return of Yarrick, and it will give you information about the different chapters of Space Marines and Ork clans that are fighting across this desolate planet.

This 114-page guide will give you everything you need to understand the greater story of the latest assault on Armageddon across the various theaters of war.

The Core Rulebook is a small-scale book, easy to tuck to the side or pass around, filled with the rules of the game. This Core Rules booklet takes a well-structured approach to teaching the game. First, make sure a player has the foundation of understanding their Datasheets, basic terms, and keywords. From there, it builds upon a battle from step 1 as you're setting up your pieces and then through each of the phases of the Battle Round.

With detailed examples of movement and combat between multiple factions and unit types the rules efficiently teach the player how quickly combat can get complicated, but how easy it can also be to resolve.

Working through the Core Rules once with a keen eye should set you up to really put it away and just focus on playing the game. Frontloading the learning experience for a better experience overall.

Assemble Your Armies

This box has all you need to build two armies. All of the miniatures are push-fit, while you might need to cut out each of the pieces you won't need any additional tools to put everything together.

There are 12 sprue sheets and an Assembly Guide that clearly outlines which sheet you should be looking at, as well as highlighting which of the parts from the sprue sheet are needed to put together your units.

The guides also do a good job of highlighting where there are multiple pieces that you can use at a certain time. For the most part, this comes down to whether your Space Marines are wearing their helmets or not. Alternatively, the Ork have color-coded instructions depending on the pose or armaments you want them equipped with.

The Blue built Boyz might have an assault rifle, green could have an axe and a handgun, and pink might have a gun and a hand grenade. There's a lot of fun opportunity here to outfit your armies in a way that best fits your gameplay aesthetic as well as playstyle preference.

Chapter Approved and Dominatus Decks

Getting into the game, you and your opponent will use the Chapter Approved decks to create a well-balanced and thematic game of Warhammer.

It starts with each player selecting their army and picking a Force Disposition Card; these cards have one of five symbols on them representing different activity types. Priority Assets, Reconnaissance, Disruption, Purge The Foe, and Take and Hold. Once both players have selected a card, they're both shown, comparing the two symbols, each player will take a mission that will be their way of earning Victory Points.

For example, Player 1 selecting Purge the Foe and Player 2 selecting Priority Assets will mean Player 1 gets the mission Destroyer's Wrath, earning them Victory Points in rounds where one or more enemy units were destroyed, if you're controlling an objective, or you killed more units in a turn than were killed by you. Player 2's mission is Sabotage, which gives you access to the special Sabotage action. You'll earn Victory Points when you commit sabotage, especially if the unit is within range of an objective or you control an objective.

While you and your opponent are still at each other's throats, these Force Disposition Cards allow you to play the game in a style that fits you better. That's not to say there won't be some matchups that might not work out in your favor, but then you'll just have to make sure you're really playing strategically.

From there, you then set up deployment, determine if you want to add a gameplay twist by adding an additional card, and then set up the rest of your battlefield and start your battle. What will determine the winner is how many Victory Points you have at the end of combat.

Where the Chapter Approved deck is good for setting up a battle for the sake of a battle by using the Dominatus Deck, you'll instead get a narrative experience.

These battles are played out over three Phases. With each phase, you'll have a Liberator (Normally Imperium) and Opressor (Orks) alliance. You'll roll to pick a different location for the phase, and in a similar manner to Chapter Approved battles will pick your Force Dispositions.

Depending on what is selected, an Agenda is selected. For a battle where the Liberators have selected the Skull and the Opressors have selected the blade, the Liberator's focus will be on the Vital Strongpoint Agenda, and your opponent will have the Repel The Foe.

Again, each army will have its own set of objectives to obtain Victory Points, and a way to achieve its Agenda, but they'll be more narratively linked. The example above sets up an almost 'king of the hill' narrative to the story where the Oppressors are trying to retain control of their Objectives.

I like the idea of allowing people to have their choice of a slightly thematic 'pick up' game where they can get into the battle fast with unique objectives to keep every game fresh, even when playing the same armies, but then if you're going to really spend some time playing there's an opportunity to build out a story and higher stakes along with a steadily upgrading force to enhance replayability and to keep things fresh.

Adeptus Astartes Units In Action

The Space Marines included in Warhammer 40,000 Armageddon are:

  • Captain
  • Librarian
  • Chaplain with Jump Pack
  • Ancient
  • 10x Intercessors
  • 3x Eradicators with Heavy Bolters
  • 5x Vanguard Veterans
  • Land Speeder

As you'd expect of the Space Marines, this faction is versatile and has plenty of options between ranged and melee combat. Some unique abilities of the Leader and Support units of this faction are Captain With Relic Shield's Finest Hour. This allows you, once per battle, to give this model's melee weapon +3 A and [Devastating Wounds].

The Chaplain with Jump Pack has Litany of Hate, which gives you +1 to wound rolls, and Exhortation of Rage that lets you have a chance to inflict mortal wounds on a target you're engaged with. The Librarion has Feel No Pain 4+ against psychic attacks, along with a 4+ InSv.

The Ancient has far slower movement, but has +1 OC, and its Unbreakable Resolve ability allows that when the model is within range of an objective or within 6" of the center of the battlefield, it has Feel No Pain 4 +.

The three squads – Intercessor Squad, Vanguard Veteran Squad, and Eradicator Squad – have the respective abilities to secure a controller objective and give +2 A on a bolt rifle attack, gain [Lethal Hits] when making a charge move, and add [Blast 1] to attacks against visible non-Monster/Vehicle enemy units.

The Land Speeder lets you move D6" after making a shot in the Shooting Phase, and depending on how you're feeling, you can either set it up with an onslaught gatling cannon or a heavy flamer.

Orks - Ready For A Big Scrap

The Orks included in Warhammer 40,000 Armageddon are:

  • Warboss
  • Bigboss
  • Bannernob
  • Painboy
  • Weirdboy
  • 20x Boyz
  • 10x Gretchin
  • Wartrakk
  • Big Mek Dakkarig

The Orks are different in that they have a few more big units, but also a lot more fodder to throw out there.

The Warboss gives +1 to melee weapon Hit Rolls, and while the Waaagh! Faction Ability is in effect his melee attacks have a +4 A. Bigboss has [Sustained Hits 1], and +1 to Charge Rolls, Bannernob has +5 InSv, and while Waagh! is active, has +1 T, and Painbow has Feel No Pain 5+ and can inflict D6 mortal wounds when getting a critical hit with its 'urty syringe against a non-vehicle.

The Weirdboy has Waaagh! Energy that for every 5 models in this unit, you add 1 to S and D of the 'Eadbanger weapon, and it can also gain [Hazardous] at 10 or more models. Weirdboy can also make a psychic leap once per turn per army. On a 1, you might take D6 mortal wounds, but on 2-6 you place the unit in strategic reserves, and it gets Deep Strike.

After that, you've got your Boyz and Gretchin, and a vehicle, and a mount. Some smaller units to flood the field and secure objectives, and bigger hitters to really put the pressure on your opponent.

Warhammer 40,000 Armageddon Review | Final Thoughts

There's certainly a lot to unpack here and a great way to get started with a new Warhammer 40,000 release. Returning to a familiar setting with an expanded lore and objectives to play, whether you want just the combat, or combat with a bit of story, this box will get you going in no time.

Snap-fit miniatures work to reduce the barrier to entry with this box and offer a nice level of customization for fans to create the army they want, or for a newcomer to understand what their options are.

Warhammer 40,000 Armageddon was reviewed with a copy provided by Games Workshop - All photos were taken over the course of the review



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/reviews/warhammer-40000-armageddon-review-space-marines-vs-orks-in-all-out-combat
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Product Spotlight: Arkham Horror The Card Game Accessories
item.content:encoded:
There's hardly a sound save for the thump, thump of the beating of your own heart. Before you unfurls a horrific monstrosity. You and your fellow investigators have put together the clues, you've got the gear, but can you truly face an Eldritch abomination... without a fancy playmat or deck box? THESE are the questions you must ask yourself as you check out our brand new product spotlight of a suite of incredible Arkham Horror The Card Game Accessories, created (and provided to us by) Gamegenic.

I've spoken about my love of Gamegenic products in the past, they're my go-to storage solution for Magic: the Gathering, and I think the quality across the board is top of class. So when I heard about a new line products specifically designed for Arkham Horror The Card Game I was immediately intrigued. Let's dive in!

Arkham Horror Game Night Chest

First up is this big beauty, the Game Night Chest. Designed to hold just about everything you need in the game in one handy box, I really love the look, feel, and use of magnets to snap the lid snugly into place. There's space for four Investigator Deck Tome boxes (more on those in a bit), as well as a pull out container for all of your tokens.

It's smart storage solutions for a game with SO many bits, pieces, and bobs, plus cards upon cards. If you're seriously into the game, with your same characters growing and moving from campaign to campaign, this could be right for you. It's not cheap, coming in at MSRP $119.99, but if you're really plugged into the game, this is a splashy, premium way to show it off and keep it safe and organized.

Arkham Horror Playmats

Next I want to show off three very cool, very different kinds of playmats available for the game.

First up are these gorgeous full-art Game Mats for the game, including a Shiny playmat (the one in the center of the above pic). These are generic, so if you're just a fan of the artwork and want to swag it with other TCG's, you totally can. But if you're looking for something a little more specific and helpful to your game, Gamegenic's got that too.

This is the Player Mat, and it has a tidy spot for each of your card types as you play the game. I'm a sucker for a playmat like this, though I recognize it's not strictly necessary to play the game. 

You and your fellow investigators may not each feel comfortable shelling out for these, though I will say the MSRP of $16.99 is way more affordable than I would have expected for the quality level on display here. It's smaller than the game mats, which is actually nice if you're all looking to fit one of these on the table.

And speaking of the table, the game itself loves to spread out as you explore more locations, so something like this Center Game Mat can be extremely useful. It takes up the majority of the table at almost 28" x 28", but this will give you the space you need to lay out all the various cards you'll unfurl in any given Act of the game.

Arkham Horror Premium Chaos Tokens & Bag

Here's another item I won't describe as strictly necessary, but if I really love a game the way I'm falling in love with Arkham Horror: The Card Game, and there's an option to swap out cardboard components for fancier ones... I'm definitely taking that option. These tokens replace the chaos tokens and bag included in the game (which, if you haven't played, is sort of how you get random results instead of rolling dice, etc.), and the bag you pull them from, and they have a great feel to them.

The tokens themselves are acrylic, and slide on the table and game mats so nicely. The bag itself is a nice upgrade too, designed to sit flat with some inlaid support on the bottom of the bag.

Arkham Horror Investigator Deck Tome

Saving the best for last in this showcase, let's check out the newly updated entry in this roundup of Arkham Horror The Card Game Accessories, the new Investigator Deck Tomes. Designed to hold your Investigator Deck and keep it organized, I'm most surprised and delighted by these.

There's a deck box designed for each of the different investigator classes, Guardian, Seeker, Rogue, Mystic, Survivor, and a neutral one as well if you prefer that. Using the same magnet tech to keep things snug and locked in place, these boxes keep a full deck safe - even sleeved, and there's a cutout window so you can see your investigator's picture card (as well as their name as it peeks through from their full character card). Each one has an MSRP of $24.99, and though I wouldn't have thought I needed one, now that I've been playing with one I don't think I would go back.

There are more new products we didn't cover, like sleeves and connector tokens, that you should definitely explore. Head over to Gamegenic to check them out! Overall, I think these all range from "nice to have" to "need to have," depending on your level of commitment to the game. If Arkham Horror: The Card Game is the kind of game you break out once in a blue moon, sure - you probably don't need any of this stuff. But if you've got a regular group playing a campaign, I think it's definitely worth it to grab some of the storage solutions and a few Player Mats at the least.

What do you think? Will you be adding any of these to your collection? Let us know in the comments below!

The products used in the creation of this feature were provided by Gamegenic and Asmodee USA.

Giaco Furino joined the TechRaptor team as a Staff Writer in 2019 after searching for a dedicated place to write and talk about Tabletop Games. In 2020, he… More about Giaco



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/features/product-spotlight-arkham-horror-card-game-accessories
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
